author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew I. 1.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “The book of the generation of Jesus Christ, the Son of David, the Son of Abraham.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do ye indeed remember the charge, which we lately made you, entreating you to hearken unto all the things that are said with all silence, and mystical quietness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Forwe are to-day to set foot within the holy vestibule, wherefore I have also put you in mind of the charge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since, if the Jews, when they were to approach “a mountain that burned, and fire, and blackness, and darkness, and tempest;”—or rather when they were not so much as to approach, but both to see and to hear these things from afar;—were commanded for three days before to abstain from their wives, and to wash their garments, and were in trembling and fear, both themselves and Moses with them; much more we, when we are to hearken to such words, and are not to stand far from a smoking mountain, but to enter into Heaven itself, ought to show forth a greater self-denial;not washing our garments, but wiping clean the robe of our soul, and ridding ourselves of all mixture with worldly things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is not blackness that ye shall see, nor smoke, nor tempest, but the King Himself sitting on the throne of that unspeakable glory, and angels, and archangels standing by Him, and the tribes of the saints, with those interminable myriads.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such is the city of God, having “the Church of the first-born, the spirits of the just, the general assembly of the angels, the blood of sprinkling,”whereby all are knit into one, and Heaven hath received the things of earth, and earth the things of Heaven, and that peace hath come which was of old longed for both by angels and by saints.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Herein standeth the trophy of the cross, glorious, and conspicuous, the spoils won by Christ, the first-fruitsof our nature, the booty of our King; all these, I say, we shall out of the Gospels know perfectly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou follow in becoming quietness, we shall be able to lead thee about everywhere, and to show where death is set forth crucified, and where sin is hanged up, and where are the many and wondrous offerings from this war, from this battle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou shalt see likewise the tyrant here bound, and the multitude of the captives following, and the citadel from which that unholy demon overran all things in time past.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou wilt see the hiding places, and the dens of the robber, broken up now, and laid open, for even there also was our King present.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But be not thou weary, beloved, for if any one were describing a visible war, and trophies, and victories, wouldest thou feel no satiety at all; nay, thou wouldest not prefer either drink or meat to this history.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if that kind of narrative be welcome, much more this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For consider what a thing it is to hear, how on the one side God from Heaven, arising “out of the royal thrones, leaped down” unto the earth, and even unto hell itself, and stood in the battle array; and how the devil on the other hand set himself in array against Him; or rather not against God unveiled, but God hidden in man’s nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is marvellous, thou wilt see death destroyed by death, and curse extinguished by curse, and the dominion of the devil put down by those very things whereby he did prevail.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us therefore rouse ourselves thoroughly, and let us not sleep, for lo, I see the gates opening to us; but let us enter in with all seemly order, and with trembling, setting foot straightway within the vestibule itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is this vestibule?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “The book of the generation of Jesus Christ, Son of David, Son of Abraham.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What sayest thou?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou not promise to discourse of the Only-begotten Son of God, and dost thou make mention of David, a man born after a thousand generations, and say that he is both father and ancestor?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Stay, seek not to learn all at once, but gently and by little and little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, it is in the vestibule that thou art standing, by the very porch; why then dost thou hasten towards the inner shrine?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As yet thou hast not well marked all without.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither for a while do I declare unto thee that other generation: or rather not even this which cometh after, for it is unutterable, and unspeakable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And before me the Prophet Esaias hath told thee this; wherewhen proclaiming His passion, and His great care for the world, and admiring who He was, and what He became, and whither He descended, he cried out loud and clear, saying thus, “Who shall declare His generation?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is not then of that we are now to speak, but of this beneath, this which took place on earth, which was amongst ten thousand witnesses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And concerning this again we will relate in such wise as it may be possible for us, having received the grace of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not even this may any one set forth altogether plainly, forasmuch as this too is most awful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Think not, therefore, it is of small things thou art hearing, when thou hearest of this birth, but rouse up thy mind, and straightway tremble, being told that God hath come upon earth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so marvellous was this, and beyond expectation, that because of these things the very angels formed a choir, and in behalf of the world offered up their praise for them, and the prophets from the first were amazed at this, that “He was seen upon earth, and conversed with men.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for it is far beyond all thought to hear that God the Unspeakable,the Unutterable, the Incomprehensible, and He that is equal to the Father, hath passed through a virgin’s womb, and hath vouchsafed to be born of a woman, and to have Abraham and David for forefathers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why do I say Abraham and David?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what is even more amazing, there are those women, whom we have lately mentioned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearing these things, arise, and surmise nothing low: but even because of this very thing most of all shouldest thou marvel,that being Son of the Unoriginate God, and His true Son, He suffered Himself to be called also Son of David, that He might make thee Son of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He suffered a slave to be father to Him, that He might make the Lord Father to thee a slave.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou at once from the beginning of what nature are the Gospels?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou doubt concerning the things that pertain to thee, from what belongs to Him believe these also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is far more difficult, judging by human reason, for God to become man, than for a man to be declared a Son of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore thou art told that the Son of God is Son of David and of Abraham, doubt not any more that thou too, the son of Adam, shall be son of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not at random, nor in vain did He abase Himself so greatly, only He was minded to exalt us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus He was born after the flesh, that thou mightest be born after the Spirit; He was born of a woman, that thou mightest cease to be the son of a woman.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore the birth was twofold, both made like unto us, and also surpassing ours.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to be born of a woman indeed was our lot, but “to be born not of blood, nor of the will of flesh, nor of man,” but of the Holy Ghost,was to proclaim beforehand the birth surpassing us, the birth to come, which He was about freely to give us of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And everything else too was like this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus His baptism also was of the same kind, for it partook of the old, and it partook also of the new.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To be baptized by the prophet marked the old, but the coming down of the Spirit shadowed out the new.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And like as though any one were to place himself in the space between any two persons that were standing apart, and stretching forth both his hands were to lay hold on either side, and tie them together; even so hath He done, joining the old covenant with the new, God’s nature with man’s, the things that are His with ours.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the flashing brightnessof the city, with how great a splendor it hath dazzled thee from the very beginning? how it hath straightway shown the King in thine own form; as though in a camp?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither there doth the king always appear bearing his proper dignity, but laying aside the purple and the diadem, he often disguises himself in the garb of a common soldier.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But there it is, lest by being known he should draw the enemy upon himself; but here on the contrary, lest, if He were known, He should cause the enemy to fly from the conflict with Him, and lest He should confound all His own people: for His purpose was to save, not to dismay.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this reason he hath also straightway called Him by this title, naming Him Jesus.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this name, Jesus, is not Greek, but in the Hebrew language it is thus called Jesus; which is, when interpreted into the Greek tongue, “A Saviour.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He is called a Saviour, from His saving His people.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how he hath given wings to the hearer, at once speaking things familiar, and at the same time by these indicating to us things beyond all hope?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean thatboth these names were well known to the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, because the things that were to happen were beyond expectation, the types even of the names went before, in order that from the very first all the unsettling power of novelty might be taken away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus he is called Jesus, who after Moses brought the people into the land of promise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hast thou seen the type?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Behold the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That led into the land of promise, this into heaven, and to the good things in the heavens; that, after Moses was dead, this after the law had ceased; that as a leader, this as a King.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: However, lest having heard the word Jesus, thou shouldest by reason of the identity of the name be perplexed, he hath added, “Jesus Christ, Son of David.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that other was not of David, but of another tribe.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore doth he call it a “book of the generation of Jesus Christ,” while yet this book hath not the birth only, but the whole dispensation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because this is the sum of the whole dispensation, and is made an origin and root of all our blessings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As then Moses calleth it the book of heaven and earth,although he hath not discoursed of heaven and earth only, but also of all things that are in the midst thereof; so also this man hath named his book from that which is the sum of all the great things done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that which teems with astonishment, and is beyond hope and all expectation, is that God should become man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this having come to pass, all afterwards follows in reasonable consequence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But wherefore did he not say, “the Son of Abraham,” and then “the Son of David?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is not, as some suppose, that he means to proceed upward from the lower point, since then he would have done the same as Luke, but now he doth the contrary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then hath he made mention of David?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The man was in the mouths of all, both from his distinction, and from the time, for he had not been so very long since dead, like Abraham.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And though God made promises to both, yet the one, as old, was passed over in silence, while the other, as fresh and recent, was repeated of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Themselves, for instance, say, “Doth not Christ come of the seed of David, and out of Bethlehem, the town where David was?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And no man called Him Son of Abraham, but all Son of David; and that because this last was more in the recollection of all, both on account of the time, as I have already said, and because of his royalty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this principle again all the kings whom they had in honor after his time were named from him, both by the people themselves and by God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both Ezekieland other prophets besides speak of David as coming and rising again; not meaning him that was dead, but them who were emulating his virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to Hezekiah He saith, “I will defend this city, for mine own sake and for my servant David’s sake.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to Solomon too He said, that for David’s sake He rent not the kingdom during his lifetime.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For great was the glory of the man, both with God and with men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On account of this he makes the beginning at once from him who was more known, and then runs up to his father; accounting it superfluous, as far as regards the Jews, to carry the genealogy higher up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these were principally the persons held in admiration; the one as a prophet and a king, the other as a patriarch and a prophet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Butwhence is it manifest that He is of David?” one may say.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if He was not sprung of a man, but from a woman only, and the Virgin hath not her genealogy traced, how shall we know that He was of David’s race?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, there are two things inquired; both why His mother’s genealogy is not recited, and wherefore it can be that Joseph is mentioned by them, who hath no part in the birth: since the latter seems to be superfluous, and the former a defect.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of which then is it necessary to speak first?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How the Virgin is of David.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then shall we know that she is of David?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hearken unto God, telling Gabriel to go unto “a virgin betrothed to a man (whose name was Joseph), of the house and lineage of David.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What now wouldest thou have plainer than this, when thou hast heard that the Virgin was of the house and lineage of David?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hence it is evident that Joseph also was of the same.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, for there was a law, which bade that it should not be lawful to take a wife from any other stock, but from the same tribe.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the patriarch Jacob also foretold that He should arise out of the tribe of Judah, saying on this wise: “there shall not fail a ruler out of Judah, nor a governor out of his loins, until He come for whom it is appointed, and He is the expectation of the Gentiles.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Well; this prophecy doth indeed make it clear that He was of the tribe of Judah, but not also that He was of the family of David.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was there then in the tribe of Judah one family only, even that of David, or were there not also many others?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And might it not happen for one to be of the tribe of Judah, but not also of the family of David?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, lest thou shouldest say this, the evangelist hath removed this suspicion of thine, by saying, that He was “of the house and lineage of David.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wish to learn this from another reason besides, neither shall we be at a loss for another proof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not only was it not allowed to take a wife out of another tribe, but not even from another lineage, that is, from another kindred.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that if either we connect with the Virgin the words, “of the house and lineage of David,” what hath been said stands good; or if with Joseph, by that fact this also is proved.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if Joseph was of the house and lineage of David, he would not have taken his wife from another than that whence he himself was sprung.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “What then,” one may say, “if he transgressed the law?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, for this cause he hath by anticipation testified that Joseph was righteous, on purpose that thou mightest not say this, but having been told his virtue, mightest be sure also that he would not have transgressed the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he who was so benevolent, and free from passion, as not to wish, even when urged by suspicion, to attempt inflicting punishment on the Virgin, how should he have transgressed the law for lust? he that showed wisdom and self-restraint beyond the law (for to put her away, and that privily, was to act with self-restraint beyond the law), how should he have done anything contrary to the law; and this when there was no cause to urge him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now that the Virgin was of the race of David is indeed from these things evident; but wherefore he gave not her genealogy, but Joseph’s, requires explanation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what cause was it then?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It was not the law among the Jews that the genealogy of women should be traced.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order then that he might keep the custom, and not seem to be making alterationsfrom the beginning, and yet might make the Virgin known to us, for this cause he hath passed over her ancestors in silence, and traced the genealogy of Joseph.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he had done this with respect to the Virgin, he would have seemed to be introducing novelties; and if he had passed over Joseph in silence, we should not have known the Virgin’s forefathers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order therefore that we might learn, touching Mary, who she was, and of what origin, and that the laws might remain undisturbed, he hath traced the genealogy of her espoused husband, and shown him to be of the house of David.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when this hath been clearly proved, that other fact is demonstrated with it, namely, that the Virgin likewise is sprung from thence, by reason that this righteous man, even as I have already said, would not have endured to take a wife from another race.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is also another reason, which one might mention, of a more mystical nature, because of which the Virgin’s forefathers were passed over in silence; but this it were not seasonable now to declare, because so much has been already said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore let us stay at this point our discourse concerning the questions, and in the meanwhile let us retain with accuracy what hath been revealed to us; as, for instance, why he mentioned David first; wherefore he called the book, “a book of the generation;” on what account he said, “of Jesus Christ;” how the birth is common and not common; whence it was that Mary was shown to be from David; and wherefore Joseph’s genealogy is traced, while her ancestors are passed over in silence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if ye retain these things, ye will the more encourage us with respect to what is to come; but if ye reject and cast them from your mind, we shall be the more backward as to the rest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Just as no husbandman would care to pay attention to a soil which had destroyed the former seed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore I entreat you to revolve these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For from taking thought concerning such matters, there springs in the soul some great good, tending unto salvation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by these meditations we shall be able to please God Himself; and our mouths will be pure from insults, and filthy talking, and reviling, while they are exercising themselves in spiritual sayings; and we shall be formidable to the devils, while arming our tongue with such words; and we shall draw unto ourselves God’s grace the more, and it will render our eye more piercing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed both eyes and mouth and hearing He set in us to this intent, that all our members may serve Him, that we may speak His words, and do His deeds, that we may sing unto Him continual hymns, that we may offer up sacrifices of thanksgiving,and by these may thoroughly purify our consciences.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as a body will be more in health when enjoying the benefits of a pure air, even so will a soul be more endued with practical wisdomwhen nourished in such exercises as these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not even the eyes of the body, that when they abide in smoke they are always weeping; but when they are in clear air, and in a meadow, and in fountains and gardens, they become more quicksighted and more healthy?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Like this is the soul’s eye also, for should it feed in the meadow of spiritual oracles, it will be clear and piercing, and quick of sight; but should it depart into the smoke of the things of this life, it will weep without end, and wail both now and hereafter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed the things of this life are like smoke.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this account also one hath said, “My days have failed like smoke.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He indeed was referring to their shortness of duration, and to their unsubstantial nature, but I would say that we should take what is said, not in this sense alone, but also as to their turbid character.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing doth so hurt and dim the eye of the soul as the crowd of worldly anxieties and the swarm of desires.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these are the wood that feedeth this smoke.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as fire, when it lays hold of any damp and saturated fuel, kindles much smoke; so likewise this desire, so vehement and burning, when it lays hold of a soul that is (so to speak) damp and dissolute, produces also in its way abundance of smoke.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause there is need of the dew of the Spirit, and of that air, that it may extinguish the fire, and scatter the smoke, and give wings to our thoughts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it cannot, it cannot be that one weighed down with so great evils should soar up to heaven; it is well if being without impedimentwe can cleave our way thither; or rather it is not possible even so, unless we obtain the wing of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if there be need both of an unencum bered mind, and of spiritual grace, that we may mount up to that height; what if there be none of these things, but we draw to ourselves whatever is opposite to them, even a satanical weight? how shall we be able to soar upwards, when dragged down by so great a load?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed, should any one attempt to weigh our words as it were in just balances; in ten thousand talents of worldly talk he will scarcely find an hundred pence of spiritual words, or rather, I should say, not even ten farthings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Is it not then a disgrace, and an extreme mockery, that if we have a servant, we make use of him for the most part in things necessary, but being possessed of a tongue, we do not deal with our member so well even as with a slave, but on the contrary make use of it for things unprofitable, and mere makeweights?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And would it were only for makeweights:but now it is for what are contrary and hurtful and in no respect advantageous to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the things that we spoke were profitable to us, they would assuredly be also pleasing to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as it is, whatever the devil may suggest, we speak it all, now laughing, and now speaking wittily; now cursing and insulting, and now swearing, lying, and taking false oaths; now murmuring, and now making vain babblings, and talking trifles more than old wives; uttering all things that are of no concern to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, tell me, who of you that stand here, if he were required, could repeat one Psalm, or any other portion of the divine Scriptures?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is not one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it is not this only that is the grievous thing, but that while ye are become so backward with respect to things spiritual, yet in regard of what belongs to Satan ye are more vehement than fire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus should any one be minded to ask of you songs of devils and impure effeminate melodies, he will find many that know these perfectly, and repeat them with much pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is the answer to these charges?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “I am not,” you will say, “one of the monks, but I have both a wife and children, and the care of a household.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, this is what hath ruined all, your supposing that the reading of the divine Scriptures appertains to those only, when ye need it much more than they.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they that dwell in the world,and each day receive wounds, these have most need of medicines.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that it is far worse than not reading, to account the thing even “superfluous:” for these are the words of diabolical invention.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear ye not Paul saying, “that all these things are written for our admonition”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou, if thou hadst to take up a Gospel, wouldest not choose to do so with hands unwashed; but the things that are laid up within it, dost thou not think to be highly necessary?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It is because of this, that all things are turned upside down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if thou wouldest learn how great is the profit of the Scriptures, examine thyself, what thou becomest by hearing Psalms, and what by listening to a song of Satan; and how thou art disposed when staying in a Church, and how when sitting in a theatre; and thou wilt see that great is the difference between this soul and that, although both be one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore Paul said, “Evil communications corrupt good manners.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause we have need continually of those songs, which serve as charms from the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, for this it is whereby we excel the irrational creatures, since with respect to all other things, we are even exceedingly inferior to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This is a soul’s food, this its ornament, this its security; even as not to hear is famine and wasting; for “I will give them,” saith He, “not a famine of bread, nor a thirst of water, but a famine of hearing the word of the Lord.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then can be more wretched? when the very evil, which God threatens in the way of punishment, this thou art drawing upon thine head of thine own accord, bringing into thy soul a sort of grievous famine, and making it the feeblest thing in the world?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is its nature both to be wasted and to be saved by words.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, this leads it on to anger; and the same kind of thing again makes it meek: a filthy expression is wont to kindle it to lust, and it is trained to temperance by speech full of gravity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if a word merely have such great power, tell me, how is it thou dost despise the Scriptures?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if an admonition can do such great things, far more when the admonitions are with the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yes, for a word from the divine Scriptures, made to sound in the ear, doth more than fire soften the hardened soul, and renders it fit for all good things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In this way too did Paul, when he had found the Corinthians puffed up and inflamed, compose them, and make them more considerate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they were priding themselves on those very things, touching which they ought to have been ashamed, and to have hid their face.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But after they had received the letter, hear the change in them, of which the Teacher himself hath borne witness for them, saying on this wise: for “this very thing, that ye sorrowed after a godly sort, what carefulness it wrought in you, yea, what clearing of yourselves, yea, what indignation, yea, what zeal, yea, what revenge.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In this way do we bring to order servants and children, wives, and friends, and make our enemies friends.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In this way the great men too, they that were dear to God, became better.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: David, for instance, after his sin, when he had had the benefit of certain words, then it was that he came unto that most excellent repentance; and the apostles also by this mean became what they did become, and drew after them the whole world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And what is the profit,” one may say, “when any one hears, but doeth not what is said?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: No little will the profit be even from hearing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he will go on to condemn himself,and to groan inwardly, and will come in time also to do the things that are spoken of.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But he that doth not even know that he hath sinned, when will he cease from his negligence? when will he condemn himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not therefore despise the hearing of the divine Scriptures.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is of Satan’s devising; not suffering us to see the treasure, lest we should gain the riches.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore he saith that the hearing the divine laws is nothing, lest he should see us from the hearing acquiring the practice also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowing then this his evil art, let us fortify ourselves against him on all sides, that being fenced with this armor, we may both abide unconquered ourselves, and smite him on the head: and thus, having crowned ourselves with the glorious wreaths of victory, we may attain unto the good things to come, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might for ever and ever.
